data.title: RAPT Therapeutics: A Buy As GSK Acquisition Highlights Long-Acting IgE Candidate
data.description:
The last time I spoke about RAPT Therapeutics, Inc. (RAPT), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "RAPT: CCR4 Inhibition Presses On For Now With Oncology Focus." With respect to this article, I primarily noted the development of its CCR4 antagonist zelnecirnon [RPT193] for the treatment of patients with asthma and atopic dermatitis [AD]. The drug was being evaluated to treat these patients in phase 2 clinical testing. The hope was that the FDA clinical hold would end up being lifted from this program.

Unfortunately, this was not the case, and the company chose to completely close down this entire program. The main thing is that it would focus on its next-generation CCR4 antagonists in preclinical development. Along with another CCR4 antagonist in its pipeline known as tivumecirnon, which is in phase 2 testing with KEYTRUDA [pembrolizumab] for the treatment of patients with gastric cancer.

It has partnered this asset with Hamni for the territories of Korea, Taiwan, and China [including Hong Kong and Macau]. It is seeking other partners to further advance this outside of these territories. Despite not delivering on its CCR4 antagonist pipeline, I'm happy to state that the company had made significant progress with an anti-immunoglobulin E [IGE] monoclonal antibody, though. In particular, it has already initiated the phase 2b prestIgE trial using ozureprubart [formerly RPT904] for the treatment of patients with IgE-mediated food allergy. The initiation of this particular phase 2b prestIgE trial was also on the basis that this potential best-in-class anti-IgE monoclonal antibody [mAb] performed well in treating patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria [CSU].

The promise of ozureprubart is that it targets the same epitope of XOLAIR [omalizumab] from Novartis (NVS) and subsidiary of Roche (RHHBY), Genentech. It has been approved for IgE-mediated food allergy, asthma, Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps [CRSwNP], and CSU. The point is that ozurebrubart is a bio-better anti-IGE mAb because it has an extended half-life. I had a Buy rating since then, and I'm maintaining this same rating. Not only because of all this progress achieved, but also because there was another exciting piece of news released. Today, it was announced that GSK plc, aka GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), would pay $2.2 billion to acquire RAPT Therapeutics to add ozureprubart as part of its respiratory, immunology, and inflammatory development pipeline.

With all of this said, the stock has traded higher by 126.81% since I last wrote about it. The beauty of the food allergy drug development program of this drug is that it can be applied to other IgE-mediated immune disorders. The key advantage being ozureprubart being a long-acting IgE with an extended half-life, allowing the dosing of up to once every 12 weeks.

Food Allergy Program Established POC Competitor, and Phase 2b Study Already Underway

As I noted above, GlaxoSmithKline entered into an agreement to acquire RAPT Therapeutics for $2.2 billion based on the prophylactic food allergy drug ozureprubart. Again, the beauty of this drug is that it is versatile in that it could be applied to treat many other IgE-mediated immune disorders.

What I want to go over in this section are two key items. One being the fact that RAPT already initiated the phase 2b prestIgE trial. I'm going to go over the design of this study for sure, but just know that there are two reasons why the company chose to initiate this study. First, there was the release of positive data from RAPT and its China partner Shanghai Jeyou Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. [Jeyou] using ozureprubart to treat patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria [CSU] from a phase 2 study. Secondly, XOLAIR [omalizumab] has already established the ability to reduce allergic reactions to multiple food allergens, allowing it to have a broad label. I will be going over both of these items below, but before moving forward, it is important to highlight what food allergens are and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

A food allergy is a specific immune response by the body to a given type of food that a person consumes. In a mild case, it can be having symptoms such as hives and itching. However, in more severe cases, there is an issue of something occurring known as anaphylaxis [swelling of the throat and trouble in being able to breathe effectively]. A food allergy is not to be confused with food intolerance. That is, you just can't handle eating such food, and it does not involve an immune system reaction. For those who are intolerant to some foods, some symptoms could be bloating of the stomach and/or gas. The prevalence in the United States is huge, which is, in my opinion, the first reason why GlaxoSmithKline acquired RAPT. Consider that there are roughly 17 million people in the United States dosed with food allergy [RAPT Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #12], including at least 3.5 million children.

The thing is that XOLAIR [omalizumab] is the standard of care [SOC] for CSU. Plus, it has been approved for other IgE-mediated immune disorders like food allergy. If omalizumab is already in the marketplace doing so well, how can a drug from RAPT like ozureprubart overcome it? The basis of this is that ozureprubart is a bio-better antibody. What does this even mean? In essence, it has been designed to be long-acting, and that is thanks to its extended half-life design. Right now, patients with SOC therapies have to be treated once every 2 weeks [Q2W] or once every 4 weeks [Q4W].

What ozureprubart can offer is the ability to have patients only need to receive therapy once every 8 weeks [Q8W] and/or possibly once every 12 weeks [Q12W]. With no need to increase dosing in CSU, plus achieving several positive patient compliance measures [RAPT Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #10], the company is well-equipped to produce a potential best-in-class anti-IgE mAb.

The first item to go over is proof of concept established by the IgE mechanism of action [MOA] by XOLAIR, which is in terms of food allergy. It was shown in a study that the drug allowed patients to take a higher threshold dose across multiple food allergens. The best-case scenario shown is the peanut allergen patients, where 67% consumed the threshold dose versus only 7% of placebo patients [RAPT Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #7]. The point is that since RAPT's ozureprubart targets the same epitope as omalizumab, plus ozureprubart having an extended half-life, it should perform better than its counterpart. The second item to note is proof of concept from RAPT and its partner in China, Jeyou. This stems from a phase 2 trial of ozureprubart [RPT904 [JYB1904]] as a monotherapy to treat patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU).

In the trial, it was noted that these CSU patients receiving this drug Q8W or Q12W ended up having comparable safety/efficacy to that of omalizumab given Q4W. To this end, its partner Jeyou is set to advance this program into phase 3 clinical testing in China. The main point here is that further POC in using an anti-IgE mAb to treat IgE-mediated immune disorders is highly plausible. The mechanism of action [MOA] has been established, and all that remains now is the execution of late-stage trial testing.

Another item to note, which was bullish, is that RAPT had already initiated the randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b prestIgE study for the program using ozureprubart to treat patients with IgE-mediated food allergy. This trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 100 patients who are to be randomized 2:2:1 to the following regimens as follows:

  • 40 patients taking ozureprubart [RPT904] once every 8 weeks [Q8W].
  • 40 patients taking ozureprubart once every 12 weeks [Q12W].
  • 20 patients were given placebo comparator.

This is to entail part 1 of this entire study, which is to last for 24 weeks. Why is that? That's because the 2nd 24-week period after the first one [first 24-week portion] is slated to be the extension and placebo-crossover period. Patients on placebo cross over to be given the drug and evaluated. The primary endpoint is set to establish the ability for a patient to consume food without dose-limiting symptoms as part of a food challenge. This is only to be evaluated for part 1 or the first 24-week period of this phase 2b study, where the drug is compared to that of a placebo. The primary endpoint is broken down depending on food allergens as follows:

  • Proportion of patients who consume a single dose of ≥600 mg of either peanut or cashew protein without dose-limiting symptoms.
  • or a single dose of ≥1000 mg dealing with consumption of milk, egg, or walnut protein without dose-limiting symptoms.

With everything I have described above, plus the nature of ozurprubart being a long-acting potential best-in-class IgE antibody, likely led to the culmination of GlaxoSmithKline wanting to acquire it. Especially for the food allergies program. The population of IgE-mediated targeting is there, as 94% of food allergy is caused by this type of mediated antibody reaction.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, RAPT Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities of $157.3 million as of September 30, 2025. This cash on hand was further bolstered by the fact that it had enacted an underwritten public offering of common stock. That is, it sold 8,333,334 shares of its common stock at $30 per share to raise approximately $250 million before expenses. The truth is that this company didn't even need to be acquired at all.

With the total cash on hand, it believed it had enough to fund its operations into mid-2028. This was enough not only to fund its company but was already set to also be in a position to initiate phase 3 studies for ozureprubart to treat patients with CSU as well. Still, if it is acquired now by GlaxoSmithKline, it doesn't have to worry about needing to raise cash in the future.

Risks to Business

There are several risks to consider when looking into this buyout of RAPT Therapeutics by GlaxoSmithKline. The first risk to consider would be the initiation of the phase 2b prestIgE study, using ozureprubart to treat IgE-mediated food allergy. Even though its partner Jeyou established positive data from a phase 2 study in being able to treat patients with CSU, there is no assurance that this will translate to positive data for RAPT's own food allergy prestIgE study. The same can be said with another drug that has been tested. Omalizumab given to patients once every 4 weeks showed broad coverage of being able to treat multiple food allergens, as I went over above.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that, upon the release of the phase 2b prestIgE study data, it will be positive. Nor that it will match closely in terms of safety and/or efficacy to that of omalizumab in food allergen testing.

The second risk to consider would be that of the phase 2 study of its partner Jeyou using ozureprubart to treat patients with CSU. The risk here is that this study, showing that this drug given Q8W and Q12W has comparable efficacy to omalizumab, was done in China. There is no assurance that the expected phase 3 study in China is going to produce a similar outcome to that shown from the released phase 2 China study. Also, if the phase 3 trial somehow happens to fail, then it will be a huge negative for GlaxoSmithKline, which is paying $2.2 billion to acquire RAPT Therapeutics.

The third and final risk to consider would be as part of RAPT Therapeutics' preclinical pipeline. The reason why is because it is in the process of evaluating next-generation CCR4 antagonists to treat patients with cancer and other disorders. The risk here is that the company already failed to do well with the first-generation CCR4 antagonist, zelnecirnon. Although this program wasn't stopped on the basis of lack of efficacy, the safety issues it faced had caused an FDA clinical hold. Thus, a big risk with the preclinical next-generation CCR4 antagonists is that there is no guarantee that they won't have similar safety issues and/or eventually suffer from a clinical hold yet again.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that RAPT Therapeutics suffered from a major setback in dealing with its first-generation CCR4 antagonist drug, zelnecirnon, for the treatment of patients with asthma and AD. However, the good news is that it had a pipeline of other drugs to fall back on. In particular, it has done extremely well with ozureprubart in targeting several IgE-mediated disorders. In particular, it initiated the phase 2b prestIgE study using ozureprubart to treat patients with IgE-mediated food allergy. The most promising aspect of this drug, which is the main reason why I believe GlaxoSmithKline chose to acquire RAPT Therapeutics, would be because of the extended half-life it has.

This allows for more convenient dosing of Q8W and Q12W, compared to XOLAIR, which is dosed Q4W. With the company having already initiated its phase 2b prestIgE study using ozureprubart to treat IgE-mediated food allergy, plus the acquisition of the company by GlaxoSmithKline, I believe that it is important to maintain a Buy rating on RAPT's stock.

For GlaxoSmithKline, it is facing patent expiration for its HIV drug dolutegravir in 2028. The hope was that it would acquire a more late-stage asset to counter this. Plus, it needs to prove that acquiring late-stage assets that have extended half-life technologies can be monetized over other currently available therapies. With all that being said, I believe that it is important to initiate a Hold rating on GlaxoSmithKline's stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Equity Residential: Markets Underestimate Its Geographic Advantages
data.description:
Reflecting increasing pessimism about the trajectory of rental inflation, Equity Residential (EQR) shares have been a poor performer over the past year, losing over 11% of their value. This underperformance has come even as EQR’s own performance has been reasonable thanks to its focus on lower-supplied, legacy markets. Given its own fundamentals, I upgraded shares to a “Buy” in August, but this call has been premature with EQR falling 4% since then, weighed down by broader macro concerns. With updated financials and a revised macro picture, now is a good time to revisit Equity Residential.

Apartment Rents Have Disappointed

The primary headwind for shares is clearly concern about rental inflation. As you can see below, rents are down 1.3% from a year prior. Rents, of course, surged post-COVID and then decelerated as supply came online and stimulus eased. Over 2024 and into early 2025, rental inflation gradually rebounded and appeared poised to move back into positive territory; however, over the past six months, it has turned back lower to run at the weakest level since COVID.

The reason for my optimism on rental inflation was focused on supply dynamics. Given surging rents and low borrowing costs, construction boomed post-COVID, and as this new supply has hit the market, rents have weakened. We have seen construction slow meaningfully, which will help to alleviate this pressure. It can take ~18 months for projects to be completed, so supply lags new construction dynamics by 1–2 years. I expect new supply to continue declining at least for another 9 months, and by H2 2026, supply should be at normal levels. Indeed, in its markets, EQR expects supply to decline by 35% in 2026.

The fact that rents have reversed lower even as the supply headwind steadily fades is noteworthy and has surprised me. This points to the weakening being on the demand side. To an extent, the Administration’s more aggressive immigration enforcement is likely playing a role in reducing the number of households and thereby housing demand. Beyond that, years of elevated inflation have sapped consumers’ excess savings and made it harder for landlords to pass on inflation. With overall inflation softening, some of this pressure may be fading, though I expect the rental inflation to be muted in 2026, running at about 0% nationally.

EQR's Focus Insulates It From Some Pressure

Now, it is important to note that there is meaningful geographic dispersion, which largely aligns with how much new supply has been added over the past five years. The Sun Belt, once the leader, is now the clear laggard. Meanwhile, the NYC-area, Boston, and San Francisco still have rising rents as they have not been inundated with as much new construction. The Washington D.C. market has weakened of late, which likely is due to cutbacks in the federal workforce. These are markets where EQR’s business is largely focused, which has helped to insulate its own results to an extent. I expect these markets to continue outperforming the Sun Belt through 2026 given relative supply dynamics.

As you can see below, EQR’s business is primarily focused in these legacy markets. The Northeast, DC, and West Coast are nearly 90% of its business with just limited exposure across several expansion markets. Notably, EQR has no material Florida exposure, and market dynamics there appear to be the weakest, especially in the Tampa and Orlando markets. This exposure profile is a clear near-term benefit. While national rents may be flattish in 2026, rents in its geographies should be up closer to ~2% in my view.

EQR Results Have Been Solid

In fact, while there has been significant new supply across the country, we are seeing EQR withstand this well given its geographic exposure and focus on higher-quality properties. In fact, its occupancy is running above last year’s level and is strong at 96.3%. That gives it about half the vacancy as the national average. Thanks to this strong physical occupancy, when the company last reported financials on Oct. 28, Equity Residential beat estimates by a penny, earning $1.02. EQR’s average tenant has a $175k annual income and is spending just 20% of their income on rent. These factors should make their demand relatively durable in the face of macro uncertainty.

Despite lower rents nationally, same-store revenues jumped by 3% in Q3, though with expenses up 3.6%, net operating income was up a more modest 2.8%. Lease rates were up 2% with new leases down 1% and renewal rates up 4.5%. During periods of market softness, renewals typically outperform new leases. Given the cost of moving, if tenants are happy with their apartment, they are likely to stay, even if they could save a few percent moving elsewhere. I expect this divergence to persist in 2026.

Importantly, EQR maintains an excellent balance sheet with just 4.4x debt/EBITDA. I am comfortable with apartment REITs carrying 5-5.5x debt/EBITDA. This provides about $1 billion of incremental debt capacity, giving EQR the flexibility to buy properties if there are opportunities to add assets cheaply from distressed sellers. Aside from this, it can be active in returning capital to shareholders. We are seeing this; in the third quarter, EQR repurchased $100 million of stock. Aside from these opportunistic buybacks, shares offer a 4.5% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure.

Alongside results, EQR tightened guidance to $3.98-$4.02 from $3.97-$4.03, with same-store sales rising 2.5%-3%. Back in August, I was targeting $3.95-$4.01 in 2025 FFO, which drove my $70 price target. I expect full-year 2025 results to come in at about $4.00, which is actually towards the high end of my own expectations.

EQR Is A "Buy"

In other words, the weakness we have seen in EQR shares is not due to a deterioration in near-term results. Rather, 2025 results are in line with my expectations. Instead, we are seeing multiple compressions as investors price in a more muted go-forward rental inflation trajectory. While the apartment rental market has indisputably softened, this softening is more pronounced in the highly supplied expansion markets where EQR has very little exposure. To an extent, I believe EQR shares have been caught up in a macro fear that is not fully applicable to shares.

Indeed, I expect EQR to grow FFO by 2%-3% in 2026 to $4.08-4.12, given modest rental inflation, a slightly lower share count, and completed development projects. I still view a ~17x FFO multiple as appropriate, given its significantly under-levered balance sheet and long-term growth of ~4% (2.5%-3% inflation and 1%-1.5% apartment count growth). As such, I reiterate my $70 price target. I view EQR as one of the more compelling options in the apartment REIT space, given its geographic positioning. I expect shares to rally towards $70 this year as investors come to appreciate the Sun Belt pressures weighing on national rent indices are not applicable to EQR’s business, enabling a recovery in shares. EQR is still a "Buy."

data.title: Nvidia: GPUs Don't Matter Anymore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

At CES, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang warned us about what comes next in AI and how this changes the market.

GPUs are no longer the unit of competition in AI.

The winners of tomorrow will be the companies that can develop a holistic system that is optimized for cost-effective performance.

This means efficient GPUs, yes, but also improvements in software, networking, and deployment.

For investors, this distinction is critical.

The next phase of Nvidia’s growth will be driven less by advancements in hardware and more by improvements in software and systems.

This is where Nvidia must maintain an edge if it wants to dominate the market.

Nvidia’s Strategic Shift

Nvidia became a market leader by being the only name in town when it came to AI GPUs.

Now, as other companies begin to catch up in terms of hardware, Nvidia is already focusing on what comes next

We mentioned that we build chips. But as you know, Nvidia builds entire systems now. And AI is a full stack.

Source: Jensen Huang, CES.

From the insights shared at CES, I believe there are three key points that will shape the AI market going forward.

  • AI systems, rather than components

  • Cost per unit of intelligence rather than raw power

  • Integration with the next layer, physical AI.

Nvidia is positioning exactly according to these three principles.

The GPU is Just the Base Layer Now

This is the uncomfortable truth that many investors miss. Even inside NVIDIA’s ecosystem, the GPU itself is increasingly interchangeable.

Value is now created at the system level, and what customers increasingly buy from Nvidia is not a GPU but a tightly integrated system.

This is why Nvidia now often refers to “AI factories,” rather than just GPU sales, and in an AI factory, each component matters.

NVLink and other high-bandwidth interconnects determine how effectively compute scales across racks.

Networking fabrics such as Spectrum-X control latency, congestion, and data flow as models grow larger and more distributed.

On top of this hardware stack sit Nvidia’s software layers, from CUDA to inference optimisation, which translate raw compute into usable intelligence.

Platforms like Omniverse then close the loop, enabling simulation, testing, and deployment in virtual environments before models ever touch the real world.

In this architecture, the GPU is essential, but it is no longer where the majority of value is created.

In isolation, a GPU is just compute, and investors need to realise this.

From Generative AI to Physical AI

Perhaps the most underappreciated message from CES was Nvidia’s reframing of the next AI wave.

AI is now grounded in the physical world, through Nvidia’s technologies for training, inference and edge computing.

These systems can be trained on synthetic data in virtual worlds long before interacting with the real world.

Source: Nvidia Blog, CES Presentation.

The last cycle was about generating text, images, and code.

The next cycle is about AI that understands and operates in the physical world and can be applied to robots, AVs, factories, and real physical networks.

Training these systems requires:

  • Massive simulation

  • Synthetic data generation

  • Vision-language-action reasoning models.

And this is precisely where Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform is designed to excel.

By co-designing GPUs, CPUs (Vera), interconnects (NVLink 6), DPUs, memory hierarchies, and simulation software into a unified system, Vera Rubin enables persistent workloads while optimizing for power, cooling, and cost efficiency.

The platform treats each rack as a single intelligent node, allowing training and inference at scale that is extremely difficult for competitors to replicate.

In this architecture, GPUs are still essential, but the Vera Rubin stack turns them into one component of a holistic intelligence system.

Extreme codesign — designing all these components together — is essential because scaling AI to gigascale requires tightly integrated innovation across chips, trays, racks, networking, storage and software to eliminate bottlenecks and dramatically reduce the costs of training and inference, Huang explained.

Source: Nvidia Blog, CES Presentation.

This is Very Bullish for Nvidia

Of course, GPUs still matter, but the true battlefield has now moved beyond the hardware, and this is good for Nvidia.

Competitors have been catching up in terms of GPU technology.

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) is now making similarly powerful GPUs, and Alphabet’s (GOOGL) tensor processing units, or TPUs, have also been a big success.

Indeed, the value of GPUs may soon begin to decrease, and the money will be made elsewhere.

What these components can’t actually do is replicate Nvidia’s end-to-end platform.

Once a customer designs their AI infrastructure, software stack, and workflows around Nvidia systems, the GPU choice becomes secondary.

The real moat is the lock-in created by the whole ecosystem Nvidia has created, which can’t be replaced.

And this is, in fact, amplified as we move into physical AI, which adds yet another layer of complexity.

Risks

Having said this, there are some risks we can't overlook.

Selling systems rather than components increases complexity and raises customer expectations.

There is also the risk of regulatory scrutiny as Nvidia’s influence over AI infrastructure deepens.

And, of course, the valuation is dependent on continued growth.

If AI spending slows, Nvidia’s premium pricing could come under pressure.

Final Thoughts

GPUs are necessary, but they are no longer the most important part.

The real product is applied intelligence, delivered cheaply, reliably, and at scale. This, in my view, makes the company’s moat wider and more durable than ever.

Nvidia continues to be a high-conviction long-term hold with a Strong Buy rating in my portfolio.

data.title: Strategy: No Longer Worth It
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The darling of digital treasuries, Strategy has lost the shine it once had. Although Bitcoin has been capturing institutional attention, collapsing premiums raise the question: Is holding Strategy worth it?

Shareholders are entitled to the net asset value (NAV) of Strategy in the event of liquidation. In 2025, Strategy's mNAV (market cap-to-NAV) gained a notable premium, peaking at a multiple of 2.4x in 2025. At that time, buying a dollar's worth of the stock would have given you exposure to $0.40 of Bitcoin holdings. Why pay for $0.40 of something when you can just buy one for one?

This isn't the case anymore, with Strategy now trading at an mNAV of 0.94; buying a dollar of stock gets you $1.06 in Bitcoin exposure. If an investor has reason to believe premiums will expand, this may be an attractive opportunity. In my view, the hype has died down. Although mNAV may fluctuate above and below 1x during bullish and bearish crypto periods, the days of 2x multiples are behind us.

If multiples don't re-expand to higher levels, the risk/reward starts to become unattractive. After all, buying Bitcoin is 1-to-1, and that comes without the added risk of preferred dividends and debt. That risk has grown in recent months, with Strategy adding a cash reserve to address this. However, this takes away from the proceeds-to-Bitcoin exchange and hurts existing investors more than usual. With that said, only investors with a strong conviction that Bitcoin will rise further should purchase this stock.

Given the current multiple near 1x mNAV, I'm giving this stock a rating of Sell. It's unlikely multiples will re-expand to former levels, and dilution concerns have persisted. This makes this digital asset treasury (DAT) more aligned with Bitcoin's price movements, and the risk-reward is an unattractive option when compared to the underlying asset.

Collapsing Premiums

Last summer, DATs ran wild, with sky-high premiums and announcements generating billions in valuation. For example, Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), the popular Ethereum treasury, saw its stock rise by over 3,000% in less than a week after announcing a $250 million private placement. This was common during the time; however, announcements today don't get the same treatment.

This hype has now died down, taking mNAV along with it. This was helped by a fall in crypto prices but was likely to occur regardless. Unless crypto prices are on a constant rise, premiums eventually fall toward 1x NAV. This disrupts equity raises, a key part of the DAT flywheel, making each raise essentially a 1-to-1 exchange from cash to Bitcoin when proceeds are used to buy Bitcoin. This continues the close alignment of treasury and Bitcoin price movements.

Previously, a high mNAV premium allowed DATs to issue shares much higher than their NAV. This benefited existing shareholders, who were able to increase their Bitcoin exposure by simply holding. This was presented to investors as the "Bitcoin Yield," which was a measure of the increase in Bitcoin per share. In short, current holders rely on new investors to increase their Bitcoin-per-share via share issuance above NAV.

After establishing a $1.44 billion cash reserve to fund dividend obligations, the yield went negative for the first time in Strategy's history, causing its removal from the company's dashboard.

Indices

Index inclusion has been one of Strategy's highest priorities, which allows it access to thousands of passive funds that are then required to hold it. That’s the biggest benefit, but visibility, liquidity, and cost of capital are all improved as well.

Recently, Strategy faced a scare on this front, with MSCI proposing a rule to exclude companies where crypto holdings exceed 50% of their total assets. If this passed, Strategy would have as much as $2.8 billion in outflows. MSCI recently announced it would continue allowing its inclusion. However, this may be temporary, with MSCI stating they would launch a "broader consultation" on how these companies should be treated.

The Strategy was also expected to be added to the S&P 500 in September, a move that would have given it billions more in passive inflows. They ended up not being selected, a move that has been widely debated. This was despite its market cap peaking around $100 billion, making it larger than 80% of companies in the S&P. A likely factor is its role as a type of "Bitcoin ETF," which would then influence the U.S. stock market's greatest benchmark.

They were included in the Nasdaq-100, unlocking inflows from indexes like QQQ, which have over $250 billion in assets. This comes from their classification as a technology firm, where a small portion of revenue comes from its software analytics. However, reclassification could come in March, when Nasdaq does its benchmark review. This is a possible scenario, considering the vast majority of Strategy's operations revolve around Bitcoin and would label it a financial company, something ineligible for Nasdaq inclusion.

Dilution

Dilution is a part of Strategy's business model, as they look to acquire more Bitcoin. As mentioned earlier, the impact on shareholders depends on whether they are existing or new and where the mNAV stands.

With falling premiums, cash raises are increasingly difficult, requiring more share issuance than prior raises. This has driven shares outstanding to increase by around 60% since last year.

Traditionally, all proceeds would go to Bitcoin purchases; however, recent declines in crypto prices have caused Strategy to build a cash reserve. As of late December, the reserve is now over $2 billion. This provides room for Strategy to meet debt and dividend obligations but also dilutes the "Bitcoin Yield" they attempt to achieve and ruins the 1-to-1 exchange of proceeds to Bitcoin. This further reaffirms the reasoning to go out and buy Bitcoin yourself, instead of being involved in other obligations.

Debt and Preferred Shares

Strategy has found that common shares aren't the only way to raise money, as debt and preferred shares have also funded Bitcoin purchases.

Preferred shares have been a strong source of funding for the company, with the potential to raise significantly more capital. This aligns with their 42/42 plan, which targets total proceeds of $84 billion through offerings. This comes in the form of four types of shares: STRK, STRF, STRD, and STRC. Although they differ in seniority, all provide a dividend, something Strategy has adjusted for with their cash reserve. With the current numbers, Strategy's dividend runway is just under 3 years, which eases near-term concerns. Note, this runway does have the ability to shorten if more preferred equity is issued.

Debt comes in the form of convertible senior notes spread out across years until 2032. This adds up to $8.2 billion in notes due, something that will eventually have to be taken into consideration. These notes have virtually no interest, with holders having the option to be repaid in full or convert into common stock. Either option poses challenges for Strategy and should be a key risk for long-term holders, with the first maturity date in mid-2028.

Risks

If Bitcoin faces extreme price changes, this could relieve or increase pressure on Strategy, which could not only affect asset valuation but could also hurt or support the ability to raise capital and fund the flywheel.

If multiples return to a premium, Strategy will have the ability to raise capital more easily and improve flywheel operation. On the other hand, continued dilution and falling crypto prices could cause further multiple compression.

Outlook

Overall, with a falling premium and rising dividend obligations, Strategy is more exposed than ever. Even if crypto does continue to have strong gains, it's unlikely Strategy will enter a period similar to early 2025, when multiples were expanding, and capital was easy to come by. The honeymoon period is over, and Strategy will have to navigate its toughest market yet to return to its former glory.

With the added pressures, I'm giving this stock a Sell. It's unlikely Strategy will outperform Bitcoin, given the measures taken to fund prior purchases. Even with a rising Bitcoin price, Strategy will face dilution to fund dividend repayments and eventually, bond repayments. All of these factors take away from the appeal Strategy once had and again raise the question: why not just buy Bitcoin?

data.title: Fifth Third: Q4 Calms Fears, But Valuation Is Full
data.description:
Shares of Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining over 13%. Shares have also recovered solidly from tumult in the late summer/early fall as the company disclosed losses releases to problems in the private credit market. These losses drove me to downgrade shares to a Sell in September, though this call was a bit aggressive. Shares have gained 6% since then, consistent with the market’s 5% gain, suggesting my prior Hold rating was still most appropriate. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit FITB. I remain cautious.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Fifth Third earned $1.08, which beat estimates by $0.07 as revenue grew 7% to $2.3 billion. Balance sheet trends were consistent with prior quarters, including a combination of slow growth and improving margins.

That said, investors can breathe a sigh of relief as credit losses returned to normal following a weak Q3. Capital is solid, and its pending M&A is set to close shortly, though I am cautious on the ultimate benefits of the deal.

Balance Sheet Growth Is Slow

Deposits were up 1% from last year to $168.4 billion. While deposits meet my threshold of “stable,” growth has been slow, particularly given its strong Sun Belt presence. Growth has been weighed down by a 40bps cut in deposit costs, as FITB strongly passed on Fed rate cuts to customers. I was encouraged to see noninterest-bearing balances rise for a third straight quarter. They are up $1.7 billion from last year to $41.8 billion. After a prolonged period of pressure, it appears safe to say that balances here have bottomed, which will be supportive of margins going forward.

On the other side of its balance sheet, FITB’s loan balances were flat sequentially and have essentially hovered just over $123 billion all of 2025. Loan demand has been muted given macro uncertainty and elevated borrowing costs. With somewhat lessened tariff uncertainty and lower borrowing costs, I do expect improvement in 2026, but loan growth is likely to still lag economic growth. This does reduce the need to gather deposits, and we will likely see further efforts to cut deposit costs as a result.

Within this portfolio, FITB has $17.5 billion of commercial real estate (“CRE”) exposure. Credit quality here remains strong with just 19bps of nonperforming loans. This portfolio is aided by the fact that half of loans are owner-occupied, making them a quasi-corporate exposure. Just 5% of the portfolio is non-owner occupied office. I am comfortable with FITB’s CRE exposure, and at ~14% of its total portfolio, FITB has less CRE exposure than most regional banks. FITB has a larger consumer portfolio, at 40% of loans, but I expect to see some credit improvement as delayed benefits from the 2025 tax bill in H1 2026 help to relieve affordability strains.

FITB has a $50 billion securities portfolio. With a 4.1 year average duration, it will take time for low-yielding securities to fully roll off and be invested at current market yields. This process will be gradual but will benefit net interest income. The bank’s net interest margin (“NIM”) was 3.13%. This was flat sequentially but up 16bps from a year earlier. The gradual roll-off of low yielding securities has helped to boost NIM, and this tailwind should continue in 2026, albeit at a slower pace.

Noninterest income was up 11% from last year at $711 million. Wealth and asset management led the way, up 13% to $185 million. Rising markets have mechanistically lifted AUM, which in turn increases advisory fees. With fees typically charged in arrears, we will see a sequential rise in Q1 from the Q4 market rally. With equities remaining firm thus far in 2026, that bodes well for ongoing revenue growth from this segment this year.

Credit Quality Stabilized

Net charge-offs were 40bps in Q4. As you can see below, there was a pop in Q4, given high-profile, large private credit losses. This prompted a renewed examination of its loan exposure, and FITB is confident these losses are not systemic of broader underwriting issues. It is encouraging to see charge-offs to return to normalized levels, adding to the hope that Q3 losses were truly one-time in nature.

Non-performing assets remain healthy at 65bps, just slightly above the long-term norm. As you can see, NPLs are down about $56 million over the past year. That said, more loans are in the early stage of delinquencies with $360 million 30–89 days past due, up from $303 million last year. I do expect some sequential improvement in Q1, given a favorable tax refund season, but this bears watching.

Against this, Fifth Third has set aside $2.4 billion for credit losses, equal to 1.96% of loans. This coverage is down 12bps over the past year, primarily due to improvement on consumer loans. FITB maintains a very strong reserve position, enabling it weather unexpected losses. Provisions are more than 3x NPLs, which exceeds my 2.5x target. This excess reserve position provides FITB with significant loss-absorbing capacity and provides downside protection against potential deterioration in the economic backdrop. I expect ACL/loan coverage to decline modestly in 2026, given my view that a recession is unlikely.

Capital Is Sufficient, But Not Excellent

Fifth Third has a 10.8% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio, which is up 20bps from last year. Capital excludes a $2.8 billion loss sitting in accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI). Just given the forward curve and pull to par as bonds mature, this loss should improve by $500 million in 2026. As you can see below, CET1 including AOCI is up 100bps over the past year to 9.1%. While not a regulatory calculation, this is helpful in considering the true economic position of the bank. I would like to see FITB operate in the 9.5-10.0% range, and I expect it to achieve this during late 2026.

Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 2% from last year, though FITB did not do repurchases in Q4 due to its pending CMA deal and Q3 net charge-offs. Shares also offer a 3.2% dividend yield. I view this payout as highly secure. After the integration of CMA, we could see buybacks resume with more meaningful repurchases possible in the second half of the year.

Comerica Deal Is A Strategic Question Mark

Fifth Third has also received approval to acquire Comerica (CMA) and expects the transaction to close on February 1st. While there should be ample opportunity for synergies given its overlapping geographic presence, I am cautious on the merits of this deal. I have been negative on CMA’s standalone financials for some time, given the loss of low-cost deposits, which will weaken its margins. Ironically, CMA was slated to lose these low-cost deposits (via the Treasury Direct Express program) to FITB, so these will all stay in-house, negating a prior net win for FITB. While I am supportive of FITB’s efforts to add scale to better compete against the national banks, I view this deal’s long-term merits skeptically.

Alongside results, FITB provided initial guidance for 2026, which includes 11 months of Comerica. FITB expects essentially no loan growth. I expect strategic trimming of CMA loans to offset organic growth. That said, we should see some margin benefits from the maturities of fixed rate assets. Most importantly, FITB expects net charge-offs to tick down slightly. This speaks to added confidence that 2025 loan losses are in the past and should give investors greater confidence about underlying loan quality.

I Would Avoid FITB Given M&A And Valuation

Back in September, I was targeting $3.70-$4.00 of standalone 2026 EPS. I now see $3.90-$4.25 in 2026 EPS, recognizing M&A can create some one-time in results. This leaves shares right around 12x earnings, towards the higher end of regional banks. For a bank that still needs to build pro forma capital and is undertaking a large deal that I am skeptical of, I struggle to view this as a compelling opportunity, especially as potential NIM pressure inside legacy CMA offsets NIM benefits inside legacy FITB. I would rather invest elsewhere in the sector, like KeyCorp (KEY) or Huntington (HBAN), given cleaner stories.

While shares are not overly expensive, I see limited upside and see shares underperforming the sector. On principle, I also struggle to recommend holding shares of a company engaged in large-scale M&A that I am skeptical of. As such, I remain a Sell on FITB. At a full valuation, even with confirming that credit quality is fine, potential overhangs from Comerica leave me cautious, and I would avoid FITB, looking elsewhere in the sector for better opportunities.

data.title: A Golden Buying Opportunity: 7-11% Yields Income Investors Are Missing
data.description:
Income investors tend to value high yields (SPYD) that are backed by strong business models and balance sheets. Unfortunately, many times, these types of stocks trade at premium valuations because the market puts such a premium on them. However, right now, the market is giving away two of the highest-quality high-yield stocks at a compelling discount. In this article, I will discuss these opportunities and share why I am buying them hand over fist right now at High Yield Investor.

Golden High Yield Buying Opportunity #1

The first opportunity is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). An important thing to note is that it is an MLP (AMLP) that issues a K-1 tax form, so investors should keep that in mind before investing. EPD is a fully integrated midstream infrastructure business (XLE) that generates the vast majority of its cash flow from fee-based contracts rather than commodity price exposure. Additionally, it has exposure to the U.S. energy export boom, which is a big part of its growth story. Not only that, but its portfolio is large and well-diversified as it has over 50,000 miles of pipeline, 21 deepwater docks, and a large processing, fractionation, and storage footprint.

The company is specifically NGL heavy but is still diversified into natural gas, petrochemical, and crude oil as well.

The company recently generated a fairly lackluster Q3 in large part due to timing factors. That being said, despite generating weaker-than-expected numbers, its distributable cash flow coverage and its distribution were still very healthy at 1.5 times.

Its balance sheet remained in excellent shape with the best credit rating in the sector, A- from S&P. Additionally, 96% of its debt is at fixed rates with a 3.3 times trailing 12-month leverage ratio and a weighted average cost of debt of just 4.7%. Meanwhile, 2026 should see the company hit a significant cash flow inflection point with EPD moving from an aggressive capex cycle to a significant free cash flow boom. In particular, its capex is expected to decline by over $2 billion in 2026 from 2025 as major projects come online.

Not only that, but those major projects coming online should also significantly increase its EBITDA. This jump in EBITDA will lead to lower leverage and significantly more free cash flow, especially given the sharp decline in capex. This will give the company enormous flexibility to buy back units, which is reflected in its recent large upsizing of its buyback authorization to $5 billion, while also being able to potentially reduce debt and even increase distributions at a faster rate than it has been recently.

This should serve as a powerful tailwind for the unit price and make it look very cheap when combined with the attractive 7% next 12-month distribution yield and a modest EV-to-EBITDA multiple of 10.25x compared to peers like MPLX (MPLX), which has an 11.27x EV/EBITDA multiple.

Thus, EPD looks like a highly compelling bargain that has been range-bound for a long time and, in my view, is like a coiled spring ready to break out.

Golden High Yield Buying Opportunity #2

The next opportunity I'm going to talk about right now is Golub Capital BDC (GBDC). It is a business development company (BIZD) that is focused primarily on first lien senior secured loans to sponsor-backed middle market companies. In particular, its portfolio is invested 92% in first lien senior secured loans with broad diversification across 417 portfolio companies, with an average position size of just 0.2%, and its top 10 holdings only make up 12% of its portfolio.

This, combined with its careful underwriting, helps to preserve capital and keep non-accruals low, as evidenced by the fact that the portfolio currently has just a 0.3% non-accrual rate on a fair value basis and 0.6% on a cost basis. This gives it one of the best underwriting performance records in the sector right now. Additionally, 90% of the portfolio is in the top internal rating categories of four or five, and only 1% of the portfolio is rated two, and 0% of the portfolio is rated performance level one, which shows that the health of the portfolio is quite strong.

Yet, the stock price has traded down recently and currently trades at a substantial 8% discount to net asset value, despite the company historically trading at a 7% premium to net asset value, given its strong underwriting history.

The reason for this is likely because the company's adjusted net investment income per share this past quarter came in at $0.39, which was exactly in line with its declared base distribution, and management's language implied that they are likely going to cut the dividend this year.

That being said, the current yield is well over 11%, which means that even if they cut it by 10-20%, the dividend yield is still going to be quite high on the current cost. Moreover, the ongoing portfolio health remains very strong, and the company is also buying back stock to take advantage of the discount to net asset value right now, which indicates that they see substantial value and are taking actions to support the stock price and even create additional net per share value through share repurchases.

Thus, a dividend reduction is likely coming purely due to Federal Reserve rate cuts and is not an indication of a decline in the underlying health of the business. Therefore, the recent stock pullback is unwarranted in our view and creates a golden buying opportunity to buy equity at a compelling discount in one of the best BDCs in the market today that also has the most shareholder-friendly fee structure of externally managed BDCs.

Risks and Investor Takeaway

Of course, no investment is risk-free, as EPD still needs to finish bringing its projects online this year in order to truly achieve its inflection point, while its petrochemical business is facing some headwinds, as are concerns about overbuilding in energy export markets. Not only that, but weakness in oil and natural gas pricing could weigh down EPD's unit price in the near term, even if its underlying cash flows are not as sensitive as the unit price movements may indicate.

For GBDC, it continues to remain interest rate sensitive, as virtually all of its loans are floating rate. So if the Fed continues to cut rates at an aggressive clip, it will continue to weigh on investment income per share. Additionally, if concerns about private credit end up manifesting themselves into systemic issues, it will likely also hit GBDC, even if GBDC will weather such a scenario better than many of its peers.

Overall, however, I'm seeing little sign of concern for GBDC's portfolio, given its strong health and impressive long-term track record. And EPD's strong balance sheet and impressive long-term track record of execution give me confidence that they will achieve their cash flow inflection point this year and allocate that capital in a manner that will create long-term value for unit holders.

As a result, I think the market's lackluster treatment of both of these equities, both of these high yielders, creates a golden opportunity for investors to load up. This is why I have been buying both in my 7-8% yielding, low-beta, and total-return outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: The Selloff Looks Overdone, Yield Hunters Are Stepping In
data.description:
Has the dust finally settled down on business development companies? Because if there's one thing that's certain, it's that while the financial sector investors (XLF) dissect the pessimism engulfing the BDCs peers, XLF has continued to advance to new heights through January. Solid earnings by the banking peers show that the US economy's resilience isn't to be trifled with. Therefore, even though the year-over-year changes in interest income have likely plateaued, the expanded capacity for loans, coupled with the well-diversified fee-related and trading revenue growth drivers, has helped propel the NII of the biggest banks dominating XLF.

And that's despite having Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) and its peers still trading at a substantial (but arguably justified) discount as compared to their XLF peers. Because when your core earnings metric is highly sensitive to the downward variability in interest rates, there is just so much that even the best management could maneuver, given the Fed's more dovish leanings. To make matters worse for OBDC investors, even as Fed Chair Jerome Powell comes under intense pressure from the White House as he enters the final months of his term as chair, the central bank is still expected to lower interest rates in the next 12 months.

For OBDC investors, the headwinds from a lower interest rate regime have undoubtedly been pretty punishing. Nevertheless, I had highlighted in my previous OBDC write-up that while the hammering was well-deserved to normalize the valuations to account for higher execution risks and a potentially weaker earnings outlook, do we really think the market is dumb and not reflecting the substantive risks in its valuation multiples?

Nevertheless, OBDC hasn't really gotten out of the doldrums, even though it does appear that the significant slide to the valley appears to have hit a nadir in November. In other words, could the worst truly be over this time, suggesting that we could see an inflection point this year, which could pave a clearer path toward a valuation re-rating from 2027?

For now, let's attempt to dissect that multi-million question into simpler parts for you to analyze and consider whether the current context represents an alluring opportunity to accumulate more aggressively or bide your time further.

After a few years of growing their loans at a noticeably faster pace than the leading US banks, the regulatory roadblocks that encumbered their banking peers have finally come off. And unless this regulatory regime gets upended in the near term, the big banks are going to leverage their well-established franchise, and this time around, press with "both hands" like what reputable Wells Fargo bank analyst Mike Mayo indicated recently:

“It’s revenge of the banks,” Mike Mayo, an analyst at Wells Fargo told my colleague Hannah Levitt. “For the last 15 years, banks have been competing against nonbanks, analogous to playing basketball with one hand behind their back. All of a sudden banks can now play against their competitors with two hands.” - Bloomberg article on private credit confidence

Hence, it's an opportune moment for the big banks to unleash a targeted approach to compete more aggressively with the non-bank lenders like OBDC. Preeminent banks like JPMorgan (JPM) are on the prowl to cash in on the private credit wave that has taken the entire market by storm. And that's despite JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon having sparred with the private credit peers on "cockroaches" last year; do you remember that? However, such a bold move arguably indicates that there are sufficient opportunities for even a big bank like JPM to flex its muscles and gain traction in the BDC arena that is enough to move the needle and make private credit a key growth optionality for 2026.

In other words, even leading BDCs like Blue Owl Capital Corporation need to navigate carefully, as more competition for customers could also weaken the prospects for the best ones. In addition, such worries are starting to percolate into the scene, as reports of lenders applying what's termed as "Covenant-Lite" clauses to compete harder for business from the advantaged borrowers.

However, while there is nothing yet to suggest that we are at the cusp of a massive default wave that could cripple private credit, such developments have arrived at a time when BDCs already need to face off against multitudinous headwinds. Thus, concerns amounting to less scrutiny on loan terms and whether such permissiveness could have longer-term implications during recessionary periods or business downcycles could return to haunt the market.

In that respect, I believe the downcast sentiments on OBDC that have also sent its forward yields surging closer to 12% are justified. Forward earnings multiples (down to 8.8x) have also declined markedly, as these uncertainties have clearly elevated the execution risks for even OBDC.

Yet, who's to disagree that the management and executives of OBDC's parent, Blue Owl Capital (OWL), have leaned in to lend financial firepower to buy up the beaten-down stock of its flagship publicly traded BDC recently, as reported:

Executives and employees of Blue Owl Capital Inc., the management company, bought $115 million of stock in Blue Owl Capital Corp., its flagship BDC with about $7.7 billion of net assets, from the start of November through Monday. The purchases were disclosed in a regulatory filing late Tuesday with the Securities and Exchange Commission. - Barron's article on OBDC stock purchase

As is often the case, I like to look at whether the actions corroborate the words/narrative. Using insider purchases as a key "action metric" to provide support to bullish views is one of these factors. Essentially, Blue Owl management indicated in Q4 last year that they thought OBDC was "performing very well." Hence, that implied discount as compared to their non-publicly traded vehicles "surprised" the management team. Thus, I believe that such purchases lend credence and conviction to the rhetoric, lifting the confidence of investors who are willing to buy the dips while waiting for the gloomy period to wane.

Next, note that the NII per share estimates for OBDC have been lowered further through 2027 since my last update. Note that the NII per share outlook for 2027 was $1.41 previously, but now it has been reduced to $1.36, a notable decrease. That has also affected the dividend payout estimates, which makes sense, right? Therefore, I think the market is simply adjusting to what has proved to be a highly persistent downtrend in lowered NII estimates. Therefore, such occurrences should continue to keep OBDC's earnings multiples under pressure, while the market also looks for above-average dividend yield to compensate for taking on such risks.

Yet, it's noteworthy that the earnings multiple of 8.8x has climbed above the November lows of 8x, suggesting that the market should have already priced in these revisions, right? That seems to me that unless the economy goes into a convulsion right now due to some unanticipated, massive, and prolonged trade war with disastrous consequences between the US and its European peers over Greenland, it's not my base case that we expect a recession of some sort that could spark an exodus in BDC stocks for the year.

Therefore, with the market confidently holding up well as observed in OBDC's price action while also defending and staying above the levels that were revisited in November, I maintain my view that the worst hammering on OBDC is confidently behind us, paving the way for private credit investors to use the pessimism to load up before the market realizes its folly for being too somber.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Netflix Launches All-Cash Bid For Warner Bros. Discovery Units
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Netflix makes all-cash bid for Warner studious and streaming. (0:14) Intel rallies after HSBC and Seaport upgrade ahead of earnings. (1:42) Gold prices surge past $4,700 as investors seek safety. (2:55)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Netflix (NFLX) is moving to further cement its acquisition of “Casablanca,” Batman, Middle Earth and, of course, The Animaniacs.

The streaming giant pulled the trigger on an all-cash offer for Warner Bros. Discovery’s (WBD) streaming and studio units at $27.75 per share.

Warner said it will hold a special meeting of shareholders to vote on the deal before April. The company highlighted that the all-cash transaction provides enhanced certainty around the value WBD stockholders will receive at closing, eliminating market-based variability.

Warner CEO David Zaslav said: “By coming together with Netflix, we will combine the stories Warner Bros. has told that have captured the world’s attention for more than a century.”

Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos said the revised all-cash agreement will enable an expedited timeline to a stockholder vote and provide greater financial certainty, plus value from the planned separation of Discovery Global.

“The acquisition will also significantly expand U.S. production capacity and investment in original programming, driving job creation and long-term industry growth,” he added.

But analysts say an all-cash deal will be a drag on Netflix EPS, even if it helps avoid meaningful ownership dilution. There are also concerns the offer could bring incremental downside, “as increased upfront financing needs could pressure Netflix’s debt profile and reduce anticipated deal synergies.”

Netflix increased its borrowing capacity to $42.2B from $34B with Wells Fargo, BNP Paribas and HSBC to fund its revised offer. The company reports earnings post market today.

Among active stocks, Intel (INTC) is rallying after HSBC and Seaport Research upgraded the stock ahead of earnings.

HSBC analyst Frank Lee, who boosted the stock to Hold from Reduce, says: “We now turn more positive as we expect the traditional servers (DCAI) to get back on a growth trajectory. We expect there is an overwhelmingly increasing demand for server CPUs driven by rising agentic AI.”

Seaport analyst Jay Goldberg echoed Lee's thoughts, upgrading Intel to Buy from Neutral, “on the back of strong signals for their PC products and an improving outlook for Intel Foundry.”

3M (MMM) issued 2026 earnings forecast that narrowly missed Wall Street expectations, highlighting the difficulty of sustaining its turnaround amid uneven economic conditions.

An EPS midpoint guide of $8.60, just a penny below consensus, suggests momentum may be moderating for CEO Bill Brown’s overhaul of the company, which has focused on boosting efficiency across its industrial footprint and reinvigorating product development.

And KeyCorp (KEY) is sliding on concerns about cautious guidance. The bank expects 2026 net interest income (taxable equivalent) to rise 8%–10% from 2025's $4.67B, implying a midpoint of $5.09B vs. the Visible Alpha consensus of $5.07B.

In today’s trading, Gold prices surged past $4,700/oz for the first time, as precious metals rallied in response to a weaker dollar and rising fears of renewed trade tensions over Greenland.

A mix of geopolitical uncertainty and worries over the independence of the U.S. Federal Reserve has spooked investors, driving flows into precious metals even after last year's exceptionally strong performance, ING analysts said.

“Investors are favoring gold and silver over currencies and government bonds amid rising U.S. debt levels and heightened policy unpredictability,” ING said in a note.

Wedbush said the Greenland tariff battle of words will cause market weakness but added that it provides the opportunity to buy ‘Tech Winners.’

Analyst Dan Ives said: “Being here at Davos this week on the ground…the tariff scuffle is clearly an overhang on the conference as Trump gets here tomorrow to speak to tech leaders and various world leaders. Our view is just like over the last year: the bark will be worse than the bite on this issue and tariff threats, as negotiations take place and tensions ultimately calm down between Trump and EU leaders.”

And in other news of note, OpenAI (OPENAI) appears to be on track to show off its highly anticipated artificial-intelligence-focused device later this year.

Speaking at the World Economic Forum, OpenAI's chief global affairs officer, Chris Lehane, discussed the timeline with Axios, noting that the device is one of the company's big attractions for this year.

Though not much is known about the product, Jony Ive and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman previewed the device in May with employees. One of the products OpenAI has spoken with manufacturing partners about resembles a smart speaker without a display. Other form factors under consideration include glasses, a digital voice recorder, and a wearable pin.

Lehane also told Axios he would have more to say on the matter “much later in the year,” and that the company is looking at something in the latter part of 2026.

data.title: Adamas Trust: Mortgage REIT Preferred Share Floating At 10.8% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Adamas Trust, Inc. (ADAM), formerly known as New York Mortgage Trust, is am mREIT, or a real estate investment trust that invests primarily in mortgages. Along with the common shares, the company offers four preferred shares and four baby bonds. While all these securities offer attractive yields, one preferred share (ADAMM) is floating above the others and currently offering a 10.8% dividend yield. I last wrote about the company and its baby bonds back in August. Today, I’m not the biggest fan of the preferred shares, but the 10.8% yield offers the best premium to income investors for the risks faced by the company.

Adamas Trust Earnings Results

The pandemic did significant damage to Adamas’ balance sheet. To avoid additional problems, the company sold a significant amount of its mortgages in the first quarter of 2020. After the mortgage market stabilized and interest rates plummeted, the company began steadily re-growing its investment portfolio.

Like many mREITs, Adamas once again found itself in a predicament in 2023. After acquiring several pandemic era mortgages at low fixed interest rates, the company saw its borrowing costs rise sharply as the Federal Reserve moved to increase short-term interest rates to battle rising inflation. Fortunately, unlike many of its peers, borrowing costs never exceeded asset yields. As interest rates fell, net interest spreads (asset yield less financing cost) began to slowly rise and currently stands at 1.50%.

Lower interest rates have not hurt Adamas’ ability to grow income. Interest income has grown steadily from quarter to quarter for more than two consecutive years, surpassing $160 million in the third quarter. Interest expense has also increased despite the drop in interest rates. In fact, in the third quarter alone, interest expense rose by nearly $20 million to $124 million.

The growth in interest income and expense is attributed to the explosive growth in the investment portfolio. Adamas has managed to continue to grow net interest income (interest income less interest expense) as it expands its portfolio in the face of lower interest rates. Net interest income edged higher in the third quarter to $36.5 million, just $1 million shy of where it was in 2021 before interest rates rose and eroded profits.

Risks To Adamas

The company’s growth trajectory lies behind the bet that interest rates will not rise. To highlight how much of a bet Adamas has taken, look at the portfolio recourse leverage, which has grown notably over the last few quarters. Higher leverage can place enormous pressure on the company’s earnings if interest rates revert higher. While Adamas has seen relief from short-term rates, long-term interest rates remain expensive, as the company recently priced a 9.25% five year baby bond.

Added to the complication of leverage is the liquidity situation for Adamas. While liquidity has remained stable in the $400 million to $500 million range, it has stayed flat against the backdrop of a growing balance sheet. Having a $4 billion investment portfolio go into distress in 2021 with $400 million in liquidity is materially different than a $10 billion investment portfolio with the same level of liquidity. The shrinking liquidity relative to the portfolio could become a problem, and management should try to address it by increasing liquidity in future quarters.

Why Not Common Shares?

Interestingly, the recent rally in Adamas’ common shares has created a dividend yield that is lower than ADAMM. But, even if the common shares sell off to a higher dividend yield, I will not advise investors to take a position. Adamas has consistently struggled to deliver shareholder value, with book value per share on a continuous decline over the last six years. It also doesn’t help that there have been three dividend cuts in the last ten years. It’s clear that if the risks mentioned come to fruition, the common shareholders will pay the price.

What About Baby Bonds?

Adamas has four different baby bonds, following the recent issuance of ADAMO. The yields on these bonds range from 8.7% to 9.25%. These are solid yields and for investors who are more conservative or have a lower risk tolerance, but the spread of 120 basis points in income yields between the baby bonds and ADAMM is too great to ignore. While the preferred shares are higher risk, I believe the higher yield is sufficient to compensate investors willing to take that risk. It’s also important to note that the common dividends of Adamas would need to be eliminated before the preferred dividends can be impaired.

Conclusion

While lower interest rates will help Adamas Trust, I’m doubtful it will be sufficient to support an investment in the common shares. One of the preferred shares is offering a 10.8% dividend yield, which is where I think the best income opportunity lies. The preferred shares are callable, and while that may mean redemption sooner rather than later, it also acts as a buffer against any short-term pricing pressure. Between the nine income options offered by Adamas, I believe that ADAMM is the most attractive.

data.title: Penske Automotive Group: A Mixed Bag That I'm Not Ready To Touch
data.description:
Back in late October of last year, I decided to downgrade Penske Automotive Group, Inc. (PAG) from a "Buy" to a "Hold." I did not make this decision lightly. However, I was turned off by mixed financial performance leading up to that point. On top of this, during the time that I had been bullish about the institution, shares had risen drastically. They increased an astounding 88.3% from the time that I originally rated it a "Buy" in October 2021 until I subsequently downgraded it in October of last year. The market, as defined by the S&P 500, meanwhile, appreciated a more modest 53.8%. This move higher, combined with weakening fundamentals, led me to take a more neutral stance. And since then, the stock has fallen by 2.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 3.9%.

Looking at the data today, I see a company that is slowly but surely still growing revenue. However, profitability remains a mixed bag, and some of the most important profit metrics have been weakening. Challenges in this sector are proving to be a problem for the company. And in all likelihood, we should expect this trend to continue into 2026. If the stock was cheap enough, I would feel fine taking a more neutral stance on it. But given the overall valuation on an absolute basis and compared to other players in the automotive retail space, I believe that maintaining my "Hold" rating makes the most sense.

Not Buying This One

Only one additional operating quarter has seen data reported since I last wrote about Penske Automotive Group late last year. That data covers the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to management, overall revenue for that time was just a hair under $7.70 billion. That compares favorably to the $7.59 billion that the institution reported a year earlier. Any sort of growth might seem positive. However, context is important here.

According to management, when we look at the Retail Automotive Dealership operations of the company, we have seen some weakness. New vehicle unit retail sales have dropped 1.4% from 49,529 to 48,809. It is worth mentioning that new agency unit sales actually increased nicely from 10,290 to 11,730. That's a 14% increase. And that was instrumental in pushing up new sales revenue for the company to $2.96 billion compared to the $2.89 billion reported a year earlier.

I do think that a bit of detail is required here because this is highly nuanced. When it comes to new vehicles, the company has a couple of different ways that it sells them. Under its traditional retail model, it purchases the inventory and then sells that inventory directly to customers. In this case, the company books all the vehicle revenue associated with that sale. But it also has its agency model, which is used by luxury OEMs. In this case, the OEM owns the vehicle while the dealer, in this case Penske Automotive Group, only serves as the sales agent. It appears as though the surge in buying under this agency category can be chalked up to not only stronger demand for luxury brands, which would make sense considering that wealthier individuals would have been more able to buy new vehicles ahead of continued tariff pressure before the year was out, but also because a lot of the electric vehicles that it sells fall under this category also. And at the end of the month of September of last year, the $7,500 electric vehicle tax credit expired, and there was documented demand growth leading up to that point. All of this makes sense conceptually.

When it comes to used vehicles, the picture for the company was interesting to say the least. Actual unit sales dropped 5.2% from 57,738 to 54,708. However, overall revenue for used retail units grew from $2.12 billion to $2.24 billion. This was because of an 11.4% surge in the average retail sales price per used vehicle from $36,785 to $40,995. It does appear as though at least a good part of this improvement came not only from a decline in the supply of used vehicles in the United States that allowed companies such as Penske Automotive Group to push prices higher, but also because of changes made in its operations in the UK that focused on higher-priced and higher-margin inventory.

There are other aspects of the company as well. For instance, the business has its own Retail Automotive Dealership Service and Parts operation, which saw revenue expand from $778 million to $818.3 million year over year. This rise in sales was driven by multiple factors, including net dealership acquisitions. However, the company also benefited from a rise in customer pay revenue, greater warranty revenue, and additional vehicle preparation and body shop revenue. In essence, management is of the opinion that the company has been the beneficiary of an aging fleet. The average age of a vehicle in the United States continues to increase over time, some of which can be attributed to improvements in technology and some of which is almost certainly because of budgetary issues that consumers are having in face of years of rising prices. So this makes sense here.

Unfortunately, the big area of weakness for the company involved its Retail Commercial Truck Dealership operations. New retail sales revenue plummeted 17.8% from $755.3 million down to $620.8 million. This was the result of a 20.5% plunge in the number of units sold from 5,405 vehicles down to 4,298. This one does not shock me. We have been in a prolonged period of weakness in the freight industry. This follows a surge in activity caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. But over investment and the economic slowdown that eventually developed into what we have today naturally caused pain for this space.

With revenue for the company rising, you would expect that profitability would improve as well. But that's not what transpired. In fact, net income actually fell from $226.1 million to $213 million. Certain operational costs, like selling, general, and administrative expenses, increased. Margins have also contracted in key areas because of broader market issues. Management mentioned in their third quarter earnings call that tariffs proved problematic. However, they did not quantify the extent of that pain. Other profitability metrics largely worsened also. Adjusted net profits saw a very similar path as net profits. It is true that operating cash flow for the company jumped from $271 million to $379.5 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $248.1 million to $310.6 million. On the other hand, EBITDA for the company contracted from $367.9 million to $357.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue increased. And actually, net profits and adjusted net profits increased as well. But some of the cash flow metrics for the company worsened. What this shows is that while there has been some weakness for a while, the third quarter on its own showed an acceleration of the pain that the company is exhibiting. But that's not stopping management from continuing to focus on the long haul. And management is not afraid to get innovative on that front. For instance, in Australia, the firm continues to focus on energy solutions activities. This particular part of the business involves the provision of power systems for large data centers, mining, and military applications. And even though it might seem odd, it falls specifically under the firm’s Commercial Vehicle Distribution and Other segment. According to management, this piece of the pie alone is expected to grow to $1 billion in revenue by the year 2030. Considering that, in the first nine months of 2025, the segment that this falls under was responsible for $619.3 million, and that it includes other assets in it, I would say that this is a rather significant potential revenue driver for the business.

This certainly helps to inform my overall view of the company. However, unless we actually start to see some big traction on that front, I think it's too early to turn bullish. If we project our results for the company based on what we have seen already for the 2025 fiscal year, we would expect adjusted net profits to come in at $928.1 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $987.5 million, while EBITDA would amount to $1.52 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. These multiples aren't bad, but they also don't make the company the most attractive thing out there. If anything, they put it near the low end of the fair value range. And honestly, that chart also confirms this on a relative basis. In it, you can see how the enterprise stacks up against five other similar firms. On a price-to-earnings basis, two of the companies are cheaper. And when it comes to the other two ways of valuing the business, three of them are cheaper.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to turn bullish on Penske Automotive Group, I doubt that makes sense at this time. The company had a miraculous run for a couple of years. Revenue is continuing to grow, but profitability has taken a bit of a hit. If the stock were cheaper, I would feel more optimistic. But as things stand, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right move.
